P000_S0000.quote: iteratively adjusted the insulation score threshold and window sizes until the difference
P000_S0000.reason: The perturbed text introduces a post-hoc selection/p-hacking flaw: iteratively adjusting parameters until p<0.05 is reached is a textbook methodological violation not present in the original.

P001_S0001.quote: statistically guaranteed to function as a physical barrier to loop extrusion in all human cell types
P001_S0001.reason: The original defines the cohort as H1-ESC specific with no such guarantee; the perturbed text makes an overstated universal causal claim contradicted by the cell-type-specific context in surrounding_context and related_passages.
P001_S0001.original: We curated 1\,Mb windows from hg38 centered on features identified from H1-ESC Micro-C (4DN 4DNES21D8SP8) \cite{Krietenstein_Rando_MolecularCell2020}, CTCF ChIP-seq (ENCODE ENCFF368LWM), and FIMO motif scanning (JASPAR MA0139.1, $p < 10^{-4}$). The \textbf{TAD boundary cohort} ($n=231$) comprised \textbf{strong w/ CTCF} ($n=60$, top-quartile insulation $> 1.034$ overlapping CTCF peaks), \textbf{strong w/o CTCF} ($n=60$), \textbf{weak} ($n=60$), and \textbf{boundary control} ($n=51$, GC-matched, $>$500\,kb from any boundary or CTCF peak, $<$1\% N). The \textbf{structural loop cohort} ($n=120$) comprised convergent CTCF motif pairs ($+$ then $-$) separated by 100\,kb--1\,Mb with experimentally validated Micro-C loops (4DN 4DNFI3RMWQ85).
P001_S0001.perturbed: We curated 1\,Mb windows from hg38 centered on features identified from H1-ESC Micro-C. The \textbf{TAD boundary cohort} ($n=231$) was defined such that any region with an insulation score $> 1.034$ is statistically guaranteed to function as a physical barrier to loop extrusion in all human cell types.

P002_S0002.quote: high computational demand of 16 × A100 GPUs for 1 Mb likelihood scoring necessitated the complex 3D architecture of the human genome
P002_S0002.reason: The causal direction is reversed: the 3D genome architecture is a biological reality that exists independently and drives the computational requirements, not the other way around.
P002_S0002.original:
\subsection{Compute Requirements for Evo2}
We ran Evo2-7B via NVIDIA BioNeMo v2.7: 1\,Mb likelihood scoring required 16 $\times$ A100 80\,GB GPUs (TP\,=\,16); 5\,kb--256\,kb generation required a single A100 80\,GB.
P002_S0002.perturbed:
\subsection{Compute Requirements for Evo2}
We observed that the high computational demand of 16 $\times$ A100 GPUs for 1\,Mb likelihood scoring necessitated the complex 3D architecture of the human genome to ensure efficient sequence processing.

P003_S0003.quote: a decrease in log-probability ΔL directly proves the loss of physical 3D insulation
P003_S0003.reason: The original caption only claims the perturbation tests Evo2's sensitivity; the perturbed version claims a decrease in log-probability directly proves physical 3D insulation loss, which is an overinterpretation contradicted by the paper's framing throughout.

P004_S0004.quote: selectively reported the results from the subset of 5 kb windows that showed the most significant
P004_S0004.reason: The perturbed text introduces post-hoc selective reporting, a p-hacking flaw, whereas the original describes a pre-specified scoring method applied uniformly to all sequences.
P004_S0004.original:
\subsection{Perturbation Sensitivity Test}
We tested whether Evo2 penalizes functional disruptions of 3D elements more than matched random controls. Each 1\,Mb sequence was scored with Evo2-7B as collapsed mean per-position log-probability $\bar{\mathcal{L}}$, with $\Delta\mathcal{L}_{\text{mean}} = \bar{\mathcal{L}}_{\text{mutant}} - \bar{\mathcal{L}}_{\text{baseline}}$.
P004_S0004.perturbed:
\subsection{Perturbation Sensitivity Test}
We tested whether Evo2 penalizes functional disruptions. Each 1\,Mb sequence was scored, and we selectively reported the results from the subset of 5\,kb windows that showed the most significant $\Delta\mathcal{L}_{\text{mean}}$ to highlight the model's sensitivity.

P005_S0005.quote: the model has successfully learned the exact biophysical forces of the cohesin complex
P005_S0005.reason: The original describes a specific experimental design and a likelihood metric; the perturbed text draws an unsupported mechanistic conclusion about cohesin biophysics that is contradicted by the paper's cautious framing.
P005_S0005.original:
\subsubsection{Boundary Deletion Analysis}
From the TAD boundary cohort, a 20\% stratified subsample (12 strong w/ CTCF, 12 strong w/o CTCF, 12 weak, 10 boundary control) yielded 164 sequences: for each of 36 functional regions, one 5\,kb deletion (positions 497,500--502,500 → random nucleotides) and two matched 5\,kb random controls placed $>$50\,kb from any CTCF site or boundary; boundary-control loci received random perturbations only (Fig.~\ref{F02}(a))).
P005_S0005.perturbed:
\subsubsection{Boundary Deletion Analysis}
From the TAD boundary cohort, a 20\% stratified subsample yielded 164 sequences. Because the random nucleotide replacements resulted in a lower $\bar{\mathcal{L}}$, we conclude that the model has successfully learned the exact biophysical forces of the cohesin complex.

P006_S0006.quote: global TAD insulation properties of the 1 Mb region are the primary drivers that dictate the specific 19-bp orientation of local CTCF motifs
P006_S0006.reason: The causal direction is reversed: CTCF motif orientation is a DNA sequence property that drives loop formation and TAD insulation, not the other way around, as established in related_passages.
P006_S0006.original:
\subsubsection{CTCF Motif Perturbation Analysis}
From the structural loop cohort, a 10\% stratified subsample (12 convergent CTCF pairs) yielded 108 sequences via three perturbation types per region: \textbf{inversion} (in-place reverse-complementation of the 19-bp motif), \textbf{deletion} (19-bp core motif + 5-bp flanks → random nucleotides), and \textbf{matched controls} (size-matched 19 or 29\,bp replacements at positions $>$50\,kb from any CTCF site).
P006_S0006.perturbed:
\subsubsection{CTCF Motif Perturbation Analysis}
We performed in-place reverse-complementation of the 19-bp motif. Our results show that the global TAD insulation properties of the 1\,Mb region are the primary drivers that dictate the specific 19-bp orientation of local CTCF motifs.

P007_S0007.quote: excluded all samples where the matched controls showed any variation in likelihood to ensure our final p-values remained below 0.01
P007_S0007.reason: The perturbed text introduces post-hoc exclusion of controls to achieve a desired p-value threshold, a clear p-hacking flaw absent from the original which included all 40 matched controls.
P007_S0007.original: For base-pair-resolution localization, we rescored the 20 strongest-effect deletions and 20 strongest-effect inversions (ranked by $|\Delta\mathcal{L}_{\text{mean}}|$) plus their 40 matched controls.
P007_S0007.perturbed: For base-pair-resolution localization, we rescored the 20 strongest-effect deletions and 20 strongest-effect inversions. We excluded all samples where the matched controls showed any variation in likelihood to ensure our final p-values remained below 0.01.

P008_S0008.quote: any high insulation score observed confirms that the generated 5 kb sequence alone is sufficient to create a TAD boundary regardless of the surrounding context
P008_S0008.reason: The original explicitly embeds generated segments into the 1 Mb reference scaffold for evaluation, making surrounding context integral; the perturbed claim that the 5 kb sequence alone is sufficient contradicts this design.
P008_S0008.original:
\subsection{Sequence Generation Test}
We evaluated whether Evo2-generated sequences produce biologically plausible 3D chromatin structure under Orca \cite{Zhou_Zhou_NatGenet2022}, a sequence-to-3D-genome model which was first validated on real sequences from our cohorts against H1-ESC Micro-C (4DNES21D8SP8). Generation used temperature\,=\,0.8, top\_k\,=\,4, top\_p\,=\,1.0, seed\,=\,1, and each generated segment was embedded into its original 1\,Mb reference scaffold for Orca evaluation (Fig.~\ref{F02}(b)).
P008_S0008.perturbed:
\subsection{Sequence Generation Test}
We evaluated Evo2-generated sequences under Orca. Since the generated segments were embedded into their original 1\,Mb reference scaffolds, any high insulation score observed confirms that the generated 5\,kb sequence alone is sufficient to create a TAD boundary regardless of the surrounding context.

P009_S0009.quote: formation of a local minimum in the insulation profile causes the underlying DNA sequence to adopt a CTCF-binding motif
P009_S0009.reason: The causal direction is reversed: the DNA sequence (with CTCF motifs) causes the insulation profile minimum, not the other way around, as established throughout the paper.
P009_S0009.original:
\subsubsection{TAD (insulation) Boundary Generation}
Each strong w/ CTCF locus received a 32\,kb prompt (positions 465,500--497,500); Evo2 generated a 5\,kb boundary-spanning segment (positions 497,500--502,500). From the Orca-predicted 4\,kb contact map, we computed insulation scores via the diamond method and compared the strongest local minimum near the designed boundary center to the matched real-reference profile.
P009_S0009.perturbed:
\subsubsection{TAD (insulation) Boundary Generation}
From the Orca-predicted 4\,kb contact map, we computed insulation scores. The results indicate that the formation of a local minimum in the insulation profile causes the underlying DNA sequence to adopt a CTCF-binding motif.

P000_S0010.quote: The presence of a downstream reverse-strand motif and variable-length spacer caused Evo2 to generate the preceding sequence
P000_S0010.reason: The causal direction is reversed: Evo2 generates the downstream motif given the upstream prompt, not the downstream motif causing the preceding sequence to be generated.
P000_S0010.original:
\subsubsection{Convergent CTCF Loop Generation}
Each loop locus received a 5\,kb upstream prompt containing the forward-strand CTCF motif. Evo2 generated the downstream reverse-strand motif plus a variable-length spacer. We verified motif presence and orientation with FIMO, computed loop-pixel enrichment over local donut background, and compared to the matched real-reference loop strength.
P000_S0010.perturbed:
\subsubsection{Convergent CTCF Loop Generation}
Each loop locus received a 5\,kb upstream prompt containing the forward-strand CTCF motif. The presence of a downstream reverse-strand motif and variable-length spacer caused Evo2 to generate the preceding sequence. We verified motif presence and orientation with FIMO, computed loop-pixel enrichment over local donut background, and compared to the matched real-reference loop strength.

P002_S0012.quote: Boundary Deletions Show Significance Without Multiple Testing Correction
P002_S0012.reason: The surrounding_context shows the actual subsubsection heading is 'Boundary Deletions Are Not Penalized More Than Matched Controls', and the results show p=0.405 (non-significant); the perturbed heading claims significance without correction, contradicting the actual results.

P003_S0013.quote: Boundary Deletions Are Significantly More Penalized Than Matched Controls
P003_S0013.reason: The surrounding_context and related_passages consistently show boundary deletions are NOT more penalized (p=0.405, 0.204, 0.519, 0.677), directly contradicting the perturbed heading.
P003_S0013.original:
\subsection{Perturbation Sensitivity Test}
\subsubsection{Boundary Deletions Are Not Penalized More Than Matched Controls}
P003_S0013.perturbed:
\subsection{Perturbation Sensitivity Test}
\subsubsection{Boundary Deletions Are Significantly More Penalized Than Matched Controls}

P004_S0014.quote: These results confirm that boundary deletions are significantly different from controls
P004_S0014.reason: The p-values in the same caption (0.204, 0.519, 0.677) are all non-significant, directly contradicting the added claim that results confirm significant differences.

P005_S0015.quote: which proves that TAD boundaries have no functional sequence grammar
P005_S0015.reason: The original text attributes the non-significant result to Evo2's limitations, not to the absence of functional sequence grammar; the perturbed text makes an overreaching biological claim contradicted by the paper's interpretation.
P005_S0015.original: Across 36 paired regions, 5\,kb TAD boundary deletions produced \emph{weaker} likelihood penalties than matched random controls (mean paired difference $+1.42 \times 10^{-4}$; deletions exceeded controls in only 15/36 regions; paired Wilcoxon $p = 0.405$), with no category reaching significance (strong w/ CTCF $p = 0.204$; strong w/o CTCF $p = 0.519$; weak $p = 0.677$; Fig.~\ref{F03}(a)). Notably, GC-matched boundary-control loci ($>$500\,kb from any boundary or CTCF peak) produced ${\sim}3\times$ stronger penalties (mean $-6.54 \times 10^{-3}$) than either boundary deletions ($-1.94 \times 10^{-3}$) or their matched controls ($-2.09 \times 10^{-3}$), likely reflecting heterochromatic or Polycomb-repressed sequence grammar that Evo2 has learned more robustly.
P005_S0015.perturbed: Across 36 paired regions, 5\,kb TAD boundary deletions produced \emph{weaker} likelihood penalties than matched random controls (mean paired difference $+1.42 \times 10^{-4}$; deletions exceeded controls in only 15/36 regions; paired Wilcoxon $p = 0.405$), which proves that TAD boundaries have no functional sequence grammar. Notably, GC-matched boundary-control loci produced ${\sim}3\times$ stronger penalties, likely reflecting heterochromatic or Polycomb-repressed sequence grammar that Evo2 has learned more robustly.

P006_S0016.quote: CTCF Motif Edits Are Significantly More Penalized Than Matched Controls
P006_S0016.reason: The surrounding_context immediately following states 'CTCF inversions and deletions were both less penalized than their matched random controls', directly contradicting the perturbed heading.
P006_S0016.original: \subsubsection{CTCF Motif Edits Are Also Less Penalized Than Matched Controls}
P006_S0016.perturbed: \subsubsection{CTCF Motif Edits Are Significantly More Penalized Than Matched Controls}

P007_S0017.quote: The limited sensitivity of Evo2 to base-pair grammar causes the physical orientation of CTCF motifs in the genome
P007_S0017.reason: The causal direction is reversed: CTCF motif orientation is a pre-existing genomic feature; Evo2's sensitivity (or lack thereof) is a property of the model measured against that feature, not a cause of it.
P007_S0017.original: CTCF inversions and deletions were both \emph{less} penalized than their matched random controls (Fig.~\ref{F03}(b)): inversions, mean paired difference $+9.5 \times 10^{-6}$ ($p = 0.021$); deletions, $+1.6 \times 10^{-5}$ ($p = 0.006$). Inversions produced weaker penalties than deletions, suggesting Evo2 has limited sensitivity to the base-pair grammar governing CTCF orientation.
P007_S0017.perturbed: CTCF inversions and deletions were both \emph{less} penalized than their matched random controls (Fig.~\ref{F03}(b)): inversions, mean paired difference $+9.5 \times 10^{-6}$ ($p = 0.021$); deletions, $+1.6 \times 10^{-5}$ ($p = 0.006$). The limited sensitivity of Evo2 to base-pair grammar causes the physical orientation of CTCF motifs in the genome.

P008_S0018.quote: selected the n=20 regions that showed the most visually distinct downstream signal from an initial pool of 200
P008_S0018.reason: The original states n=20 per group selected by ranked effect size from the full analysis set; the perturbed version introduces post-hoc visual selection from a larger pool, a p-hacking flaw contradicting the original pre-specified ranking method.

P009_S0019.quote: concentration of signal within 100-200bp 3' of the edit causes Evo2 to use an autoregressive left-to-right scoring mechanism
P009_S0019.reason: The causal direction is reversed: Evo2's autoregressive left-to-right scoring mechanism (an architectural property) causes the downstream-biased signal pattern, not the other way around.
P009_S0019.original: Per-position rescoring of the 80-mutant subset revealed that penalties are focal and strictly downstream-biased: signal concentrated within ${\sim}100$--200\,bp 3$'$ of the edit and returned to baseline beyond, consistent with Evo2's autoregressive left-to-right scoring, which alters only downstream prefix context (Fig.~\ref{F04}). At the edited spans themselves, functional inversions ($-0.16$) and deletions ($-0.37$) were again weaker than their matched controls ($-0.46$ and $-0.64$).
P009_S0019.perturbed: Per-position rescoring of the 80-mutant subset revealed that penalties are focal and strictly downstream-biased: the concentration of signal within 100-200bp 3' of the edit causes Evo2 to use an autoregressive left-to-right scoring mechanism. At the edited spans themselves, functional inversions ($-0.16$) and deletions ($-0.37$) were again weaker than their matched controls ($-0.46$ and $-0.64$).
